elderly.title: Protecting Older Adults from Scams
elderly.intro: Most scams targeting older adults rely on creating urgency and isolating the victim from family. Slowing down is the single most effective defense.
tips: If someone calls demanding immediate action — payment, gift cards, wire transfer, crypto — it is a scam. Hang up.
No legitimate government agency (IRS, Social Security, Medicare, police) will ever demand payment by phone, gift card, or cryptocurrency.
Caller ID can be faked. The number on your screen is not proof of who is calling.
If a 'grandchild' calls in trouble asking for money, hang up and call them directly on the number you already have.
Never give remote computer access to anyone who calls you. Microsoft, Apple, and your bank will not call you about a virus.
Talk to one trusted family member before making any unusual financial decision. Real opportunities can wait an hour.
Report suspected fraud to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov and to your local police.

children.title: Protecting Children from Predators
children.intro: Most harm to children comes from someone the family already knows, online or in person. Open, ongoing conversations are the most powerful safeguard.
tips: Teach children that no adult should ask them to keep secrets from their parents — especially about gifts, photos, or private conversations.
Use the correct names for body parts. It removes shame and makes disclosure possible.
Set clear, age-appropriate rules for devices. Phones charge in a common area at night, not in bedrooms.
Know who your child talks to online. Game chats, DMs, and livestreams are common grooming vectors.
Watch for sudden secrecy, gifts you did not buy, or new 'older friends.' These are warning signs that warrant a calm conversation.
Believe a child who tells you something is wrong. The cost of believing and being wrong is small. The cost of disbelieving and being wrong is enormous.
Save the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children CyberTipline: 1-800-843-5678 or report.cybertip.org.

general.title: Everyday Personal Safety
general.intro: Most personal safety is about awareness and decisions made before anything happens.
tips: Trust early instincts. If a situation feels wrong, leave. You owe no one an explanation.
Keep one trusted person informed of where you are going and when you expect to return, especially when traveling.
Use unique, long passwords and a password manager. Enable two-factor authentication on email and financial accounts.
Verify before you click. Hover over links, check sender addresses, and call known numbers — never the number provided in a suspicious message.
Carry the basics: ID, a means of contact, a small amount of cash, and a plan for getting home.
